research_question: Does AI review assistance systematically shift mid-level reviewers' attention toward locally-checkable surface issues and away from architectural or convention-specific concerns that require tribal knowledge of a legacy codebase?
intervention_primitive: seeded-defect pull requests spanning a taxonomy of bug classes (local syntax/logic, cross-file coupling, project-convention violations, architectural drift) injected into forks of real open-source legacy repositories, reviewed with vs. without an AI review assistant
one_sentence_claim: Mid-level reviewers with AI assistance catch more local defects but miss more architectural and convention-specific defects than unaided reviewers on matched legacy pull requests, with the shift being largest where project-specific context is thickest.
why_divergent: Differs from branch B on human_system_relationship (system_augments_human vs adversarial_check) and intervention_primitive (seeded-defect PR corpus vs a red-team agent challenging the reviewer); differs from branch C on method_family (evaluative vs longitudinal) and research_question (attention-shift in bug-catching vs mental-model formation over time).

grounding.quoted_claim: AI decision-support can erode domain-specific autonomy via absent failure indicators and unconscious value shifts, undermining the skilled competence component of expertise.
LLM-generated feedback overlaps with human reviewer feedback at roughly 30–39%, suggesting AI assistance complements but does not replicate the full scope of human expert review judgment.

Structural and technological features of a system can bias credibility judgments via cognitive heuristics triggered by surface cues, in ways users do not notice — carrying implications for how interface design shapes attention and evaluation.

Simulated reviewer agent biases can account for substantial variation in review outcomes, illustrating how latent factors in reviewer cognition and support tools systematically shift which issues receive attention.
